does: This paper introduces OmniSafeBench-MM, a comprehensive benchmark and toolbox for evaluating multimodal jailbreak attacks and defenses in large vision-language models. The benchmark integrates 13 representative attack methods and 15 defense strategies, evaluated against a diverse dataset spanning 9 risk domains with 50 fine-grained categories. Attacks are structured across three inquiry types—consultative, imperative, and declarative—to reflect realistic user intentions. The evaluation protocol operates along three dimensions: harmfulness measured on a granular multi-level scale, robustness across different attack modalities, and model utility preservation. The paper provides standardized metrics and experimental results demonstrating which attack-defense combinations are most effective, offering practitioners reproducible tools for assessing and improving MLLM safety.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to systematize multimodal safety research, extending prior benchmarks like JailBreakV-28K, MM-SafetyBench, and HADES that identified vulnerabilities in vision-language models but operated in isolation with limited attack scenarios and inconsistent evaluation protocols. It synthesizes insights from the broader jailbreak literature spanning text-only and image-based attacks while acknowledging that multimodal systems present unique attack surfaces through visual-linguistic fusion. The work connects to established safety evaluation frameworks in NLP while adapting them to multimodal contexts, building on decades of adversarial robustness research and more recent vision-language model alignment studies that showed MLLMs inherit vulnerabilities from both component modalities.
enables: By providing a unified, reproducible toolbox with standardized metrics, this benchmark enables systematic comparison of attack and defense approaches, facilitating identification of which defense strategies generalize across attack types and threat models. The three-dimensional evaluation protocol supports more nuanced understanding of the robustness-utility tradeoff, helping researchers design defenses that maintain model capability while reducing vulnerability. The fine-grained categorization across risk domains and inquiry types creates opportunities for developing domain-specific or intent-aware defenses. Future work can build on this foundation to explore compositional attacks, cross-modal attack patterns, or adaptive defenses that learn from attack patterns in the benchmark, ultimately advancing toward provably robust multimodal systems.
